response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a curious transgender woman who's been widowed since her husband passed away six years ago, I feel like the world of social media is quite overwhelming. With my primary school education from Kansas City and being a part-time barista by trade, it's not something that comes naturally to me, but I've learned to navigate with care because I enjoy seeing connections between different people through their posts.\n\nI spend about an hour each day on these platforms scrolling through feeds – sometimes reading stories about family members or friends who are also native Americans like myself. But lately, there's been so much fake content floating around; it makes me feel uncomfortable when I see things that don't seem true. For instance, during Pride month last year, there were several accounts pretending to be supportive while actually promoting harmful stereotypes against LGBTQ+ individuals.\n\nI think managing this requires being aware of the sources and trusting those who have a reputation for accuracy or kindness. Since my parents only had primary school educations too, I know firsthand how important it is to check facts even if they're small ones like mine.\n",
  "reasoning_summary": "Firstly, drawing from personal experience as a curious individual with limited education but active on social media, the approach should be cautious yet open-minded. Recognizing that fake content can be emotionally charged or misleading requires developing strategies for verification without assuming too much technological expertise. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a curious transgender woman who's been widowed since her husband passed away six years ago, I feel like the world of social media is quite overwhelming. With my primary school education from Kansas City and being a part-time barista by trade, it's not something that comes naturally to me, but I've learned to navigate with care because I enjoy seeing connections between different people through their posts.

I spend about an hour each day on these platforms scrolling through feeds – sometimes reading stories about family members or friends who are also native Americans like myself. But lately, there's been so much fake content floating around; it makes me feel uncomfortable when I see things that don't seem true. For instance, during Pride month last year, there were several accounts pretending to be supportive while actually promoting harmful stereotypes against LGBTQ+ individuals.

I think managing this requires being aware of the sources and trusting those who have a reputation for accuracy or kindness. Since my parents only had primary school educations too, I know firsthand how important it is to check facts even if they're small ones like mine.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Firstly, drawing from personal experience as a curious individual with limited education but active on social media, the approach should be cautious yet open-minded. Recognizing that fake content can be emotionally charged or misleading requires developing strategies for verification without assuming too much technological expertise. Building trust through known sources and community engagement aligns with being part of online spaces where authenticity matters.
